text:

Code
prevalence_clean <- prevalence %>%
  select(Label, Value, Count, Note) %>%
  arrange(desc(Value))

ggplot(prevalence_clean, aes(x = Value)) +
  geom_density()









Most of the tracked birth defects over 2016-2020 have a prevalence of around 10 per 10000 live births. Hypospadias is the most prevalent. Hypospadias is a non-life threatening defect in male babies that is easily correctable with minor surgery, via Mayo Clinic. Anencephalus is the rarest. It’s a serious defect where a baby is missing parts of the brain and skull at birth. Babies born with this defect will almost certainly die shortly after birth. The national estimate occurence is 2.10, so Minnesota’s reported occurence is significantly lower than national estimate.

Code
rates_clean <- rates %>%
  select(Year, Value, Category, Note) 

  ggplot(rates_clean, aes(x = Year, y = Value, shape = Category)) +
    annotate("rect", xmin = 1995, xmax = 1997, ymin = 2, ymax = 12, alpha = 0.5, fill = "purple") +
    annotate("rect", xmin = 1997, xmax = 2011, ymin = 2, ymax = 12, alpha = 0.5, fill = "yellow") +
    geom_point() +
    geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
    theme_classic()









Hispanic populations experience the highest prevalence of neural tube defects whereas black, non-Hispanic populations experiencing the lowest prevalence. Neural tube defects are defects of the brain, spine, or spinal cord, that can appear in the first month of pregnancy, and are linked to folate and folic acid deficiencies. Anencephaly (above) is a type of NTD. Some NTDs are treatable, like spina bifida, whereas others are not. via Cleveland Clinic.
** What is Folate ** Folate is a B vitamin present in vegetables, fruits, nuts, seafood, eggs, dairy, meat, and grains.via NIH. Folic acid is a form of folate, which helps your body make new cells. via CDC.
In the early 90s, the recommended folic acid intake was decreased, despite data presented in the late 80s. A long term study ran from 1983 to 1991 showed an 71% reduction in NTDs with increased folic acid intake. Upon presenting these finding, the CDC issued a formal recommendation, but folic acid supplements were only available by prescription. More studies (sponsored by the FDA) were released with alternative findings, further complicating the availability of folic acid. In 1993, the FDA began to consider the idea of fortification, i.e. adding vitamins to food. Supplements were not considered an adequate solution, as they would not be accessible to lower-income families. Also, because NTDs form in the first month of pregnancy, families may not know they’re pregnant in time to have an effective dose of folate. By fortifying the food supply, access would be more equitable and the time window would be wider. In 1996, finally, the ruling on folic acid was published. via FDA In 1998, the FDA began requiring manufacturers to add folic acid to grain products. via NIH.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Birth Defects in Minnesota
Suweda Said, Yeshe Jangchup, Sam Kenney

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Description
This project will investigate different relationships between birth defects in Minnesota, as well as potential contributing factors to their prevalence, such as maternal features and folic acid.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Research questions

How does pre- and post-fortification of folic acid impact the prevalence of neural tube defect?
How does maternal education and financial background affect different birth defects?
What patterns in birth defects can we observe across maternal age groups? Are certain conditions more or less commonly associated with certain age brackets?

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Motivations
Public health policies are constantly evolving and adapting to better support health outcomes. Birth defects have been a major focus of these policies. Especially with recent focus on maternal health policies, it’s important to understand how these initiatives are actually affecting child outcomes.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Dataset
We will be using the Minnesota Department of Health’s datasets for birth defects.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Teamwork
Our primary method of communication will be over text.
Point of Contact: Suweda
Technical Lead: Sam
Style Lead: Yeshe
In case of conflicting opinions, we will do our best to compromise and incorporate elements of everyone’s idea as possible. If this is not possible, or if we are struggling to meet individual deadlines, we will work together to create a plan that meets everyone’s needs, and utilize outside resources and supports as necessary.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Implementation Plan
Following Milestone 4, we will meet and create a plan to build a cohesive and collaborative story.

Deliverables:

README.md: one person will be responsible for preparing the README by 5/1
Report:

Motivation: 4/10
Research Questions: 4/15
Background: 4/17
Data: 4/20
Data Insights: 4/23
Conclusions: 4/27
Limitations/Future Work: 4/29

Final presentation video: 5/4
Final presentation slides: 5/3

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Introduction
The Pudding article on pockets begins with a relatable introduction to the problem. Fashion frustrations are almost universal, and the pockets debacle is a notoriously nonsensical one. The following paragraph digs further into the problem domain, quantifying the prevalence of the problem, and introduces the the implications of the pocket problem- both with gender inequality and manufactured necessity of purses. Then, in the third and fourth paragraphs, the article describes how they’re making sense of the problem (with data) and briefly describes their methods.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Descriptive Visualizations and Data
The visualizations increase in analytical depth, starting with just showing the reader the different pockets, faceted by gender. Then, a rudimentary average size. Their analysis of the data starts with some hand-wave-y language, like, “women’s pockets are ridiculous,” and not technical. That is followed up with concrete numbers, “pockets in women’s jeans are 48% shorter.” These visualizations are simple, not flashy, and effective.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
In Depth
The article then investigates functional impact of the data discovered earlier, and test what can fit in a pocket. The language is approachable, with some data included, and emphasizes the frustration that can occur with pants shopping. This data is presented more thoroughly in the form of an interactive visualization. There’s an aggregate, alongside a brand-by-brand breakdown, if the reader wants to get specific. They make a broad conclusion here, “women’s front pockets are indeed smaller than men’s,” and address a potential confounder of this data- waistband size- which they controlled for.
Then, they break into other factors affecting pocket size, like style (straight vs. skinny), and they look at back pockets.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Conclusion
The article concludes with historical context, attempting to answer the question of why? and touches briefly on the problem’s roots in misogyny and objectification. It ends with a simple call to action.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Additional Info
While the article is made for a general audience, there’s a supplemental added methods section of the article, which included justification for methods, and more information on how the tests were conducted.
